sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Protein P is normally secreted after entering the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), passing through the Golgi, and traveling in vesicles to the plasma membrane. Deleting its N-terminal signal peptide caused cytosolic retention and loss of secretion. Brefeldin A caused ER accumulation. A lysosome inhibitor increased intracellular vesicle signal for a mutant Protein P that is normally degraded rapidly.
questions.stem: What is the most likely role of the N-terminal signal peptide?

choices.D: Targeting nascent Protein P to the ER for secretion
choices.A: Binding DNA in the nucleus to activate transcription
choices.B: Hydrolyzing ATP as a motor protein
choices.C: Marking mature secreted protein for translation on free ribosomes only

explanations.D: Correct. Secreted proteins commonly use signal peptides to direct ribosomes to the rough ER and enter the secretory pathway.
explanations.A: The signal peptide described affects localization, not DNA binding.
explanations.B: The passage does not describe a motor ATPase function.
explanations.C: Secreted proteins are generally translated into the ER, not only on free cytosolic ribosomes.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Protein P localization after trafficking perturbations.

questions.stem: Brefeldin A causing ER accumulation most directly suggests impaired transport from:

explanations.A: Correct. ER accumulation when brefeldin A is present indicates blockade of ER-to-Golgi trafficking.
explanations.B: Protein P is secretory-pathway cargo, not a nuclear mitochondrial cargo.
explanations.C: The ER/Golgi pathway is implicated, not lysosome-to-peroxisome transport.
explanations.D: Ribosomes are not destinations for cytosolic transport.

questions.stem: The lysosome inhibitor result for mutant Protein P implies that the mutant is normally:

choices.A: Secreted more efficiently than wild-type Protein P
choices.B: Converted into DNA in the Golgi
choices.D: Used as a plasma membrane ion channel exclusively

explanations.C: Correct. Increased intracellular vesicle signal after lysosome inhibition indicates the mutant is usually degraded in lysosomes.
explanations.A: Inhibition increasing intracellular signal suggests reduced degradation, not better secretion.
explanations.B: Proteins are not converted into DNA in the Golgi.
explanations.D: The passage describes degradation, not exclusive ion channel function.

questions.stem: Which cellular structure is most directly responsible for co-translational entry of secreted proteins?

explanations.B: Correct. Ribosomes bound to rough ER translate many secreted and membrane proteins into the ER lumen or membrane.
explanations.A: Sarcomeres mediate contraction, not secretory protein translocation.
explanations.C: The nucleolus makes ribosomal RNA and assembles ribosomal subunits.
explanations.D: Centrosomes organize microtubules and spindle poles.

questions.stem: Which experiment would best test whether Protein P is secreted?

choices.A: Measure Protein P in culture medium by immunoblot or ELISA
choices.C: Count mitochondrial cristae in treated cells
choices.D: Stain actin filaments without detecting Protein P

explanations.A: Correct. Detecting Protein P in the extracellular medium directly assays secretion.
explanations.B: DNA concentration does not report Protein P secretion.
explanations.C: Mitochondrial structure is not the secretory readout.
explanations.D: Actin staining alone does not detect Protein P localization or secretion.

questions.passage: Stem-like cells were treated with differentiation factors and analyzed by flow cytometry. Marker 1 indicates an early lineage state, Marker 2 indicates a later lineage state, and double-positive cells are transitioning. Factor X increased Marker 2-only cells, Factor Y increased double-positive cells, and the combination produced the largest double-positive population.
questions.stem: Which treatment produced the largest transitioning population?

explanations.D: Correct. The double-positive percentage is highest in the X + Y condition in the figure.
explanations.A: Untreated cells are mostly Marker 1 only.
explanations.B: Factor X mainly increases Marker 2-only cells, not the largest double-positive group.
explanations.C: Factor Y increases double-positive cells, but less than X + Y.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Marker expression measured by flow cytometry after differentiation treatments.

questions.stem: In a two-marker flow cytometry plot, double-positive cells would fall in the quadrant with:

choices.C: High Marker 1 and high Marker 2 fluorescence
choices.A: Low Marker 1 and low Marker 2 fluorescence
choices.B: High forward scatter but no fluorescence

explanations.C: Correct. Double-positive cells have fluorescence above threshold for both markers.
explanations.B: Forward scatter estimates size and does not itself define marker positivity.
explanations.D: Dead-cell dye positivity is a viability readout, not the two lineage markers.

questions.stem: Factor X alone most strongly promotes:

choices.B: Progression toward the later Marker 2-only lineage state
choices.A: Maintenance of the untreated early state
choices.C: Complete elimination of all differentiated cells

explanations.B: Correct. Factor X increases Marker 2-only cells, which the passage defines as the later lineage state.
explanations.A: Untreated cells are mostly Marker 1 only, while Factor X shifts away from that.
explanations.C: The data show cells in marker-defined states, not eliminated cells.
explanations.D: Proteins are not converted into mRNA.

questions.stem: Why is total cell number an important additional control in this experiment?

choices.D: Marker percentages could change if treatments selectively kill one population
choices.A: It proves all cells have identical genomes with no mutations
choices.B: It replaces the need for fluorescence compensation

explanations.D: Correct. A treatment could appear to alter differentiation percentages by selectively affecting survival, so total cell number/viability helps interpret the marker data.
explanations.A: Cell counts do not prove absence of mutations.
explanations.B: Fluorescence compensation is still needed for multicolor flow cytometry.
explanations.C: Flow marker percentages do not directly measure secretory trafficking.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the combination treatment?

choices.A: Factors X and Y together favor a transitional double-positive state more than either alone
choices.B: Factors X and Y prevent any lineage-marker expression
choices.C: Factor Y abolishes all effects of Factor X
choices.D: Untreated cells are mostly late-stage Marker 2-only cells

explanations.A: Correct. The combination produces the highest double-positive percentage, supporting a stronger effect on the transitional state.
explanations.B: The combination increases marker-positive cells rather than preventing marker expression.
explanations.C: The combined effect is not abolished; it is enhanced for double-positive cells.
explanations.D: Untreated cells are mostly Marker 1 only.
